authors.name: Haddis Alemayehu
authors.bio: Haddis Alemayehu was an Ethiopian novelist. His Amharic novel Love to the Grave is considered a classic of modern Ethiopian literature. Aside from literature, he was the Minister of Foreign Affairs from 1960 to 1961.

books.title: Fikir Eske Mekabir (Love until Death)
books.description: It is one of the best known novels in Ethiopia and is considered a classic of Ethiopian literature featuring a tragic love story.

books.title: Tizzita (Memories)
books.description: It is a book that describes the life they lived in exile during the five-year Italian occupation.

books.title: Yelimzhat (Sweet Only in Dreams)
books.description: The main character of this "Yelm Zhat" book is a beautiful woman named Demekech Tsehay. Haddis shows us how this beautiful character was forced from the countryside and came to our capital, Addis Ababa, what kind of difficult life she lives in, and how she manages to earn a living by educating her only child (at the Teferi Mekonnen School).

books.title: Wenjelegnaw Dagna (The Criminal Judge)
books.description: "Whether I started Wenjelegnaw Dagna as a play or finished it as a novel, my real goal was to portray the problems facing the Ethiopian working class of the time and the generation that is graduating from modern schools in that working class, as I saw them, in words. Whether I have achieved this goal or not, the readers are the judges." -The Author

books.title: Teret Teret Yemeseret
books.description: Haddis Alemayehu's first published book. It contains sixteen tales, most of which have animals as their main characters; even if humans appear in the tales, they are portrayed as having not so much significant role. Tales in which abstract ideas such as death, old age, and disease are embodied are also included in the collection.

books.title: Ethiopia mn aynet astedader yasfeligatal?
books.description: Taking the December 1953 uprising, which was considered unprecedented in Ethiopia, as a starting point, he explained that the existing administrative system in Ethiopia before the coup was no longer viable, and he justified the need for change, but he also analyzed in detail how the change would benefit our country and its people. "What Kind of Administration Does Ethiopia Need?" (1958)

books.title: Yetmirt ena yetemari bet tirgum
books.description: "I do not claim that this small book, bearing the grand title 'The Meaning of Education and the School,' will fully explain what a school and education are. But I hope that it will be sufficient to give a brief general idea of education and the school, and to stimulate the minds of readers to follow the extensive research that has been done on these two main subjects in detail and carefully. " -The author

books.title: Yeabesha ena Yewedehuala gabicha
books.description: The novel explores generational conflict, marriage customs, and the pull between progress and conservatism. Through its characters, it contrasts Western-influenced modernization with Ethiopia’s deep-rooted traditions. Alemayehu’s prose is known for accessible yet poetic Amharic, vivid dialogue, and moral introspection.

authors.name: Adam Reta
authors.bio: Adam Reta was born in Addis Ababa in 1950 EC. He received his first degree in Geography from Addis Ababa University. He also received his second (master's) degree abroad. He writes mostly novels, but he also writes poems. His poems have been published in various magazines at different times. He also has unpublished poems.

books.title: Mahlet
books.description: "I read this book twice. Both times it shook my mind and shook it deeply. Dialectics teaches us that the transition (the final moment of transition) is called progress. Adam Reta's collection of short stories 'Mahlet' is the progress of the Amharic short stories. ...To understand the structure of these gem short stories, one must read them from within." -Abdella Azra-Sana

books.title: Merek
books.description: "Merek" is a popular novel written by the famous author Adam Reta and published in 2007 (2014/15). The book reflects Adam Reta's unique writing style and philosophical horizons, and contains the following main points. The book explores the social and political atmosphere during the Ethiopian revolution of the 1960s and 70s. It narrates the life journey of Alazar, his childhood memories, and the tension that prevailed in the aftermath of the revolution. The book analyzes human existence, alienation, and the meaning of life from a "realist" perspective.

authors.name: Sisay Negusu
authors.bio: An acclaimed Ethiopian fiction writer recognized for his deep emotional storytelling and influence on modern Amharic literature. His works explore themes of love, social change, and personal identity, earning him a lasting place in Ethiopia’s literary culture.

authors.name: Mehret Debebe
authors.bio: Dr. Mihret Debebe is a psychiatrist by profession. He received his medical degree from Addis Ababa University and later went to the United States to complete his psychiatry studies at Saint Louis University. He currently works between the United States and Ethiopia, practicing in both places. In addition to his medical practice, he continues to think, study, and teach about the world of thought and mental transformation. He has published two books so far, in 2005 and 2007, entitled "Yeteqolefebet Kulf (A Key that got Locked)" and "Lela Sew (Other Person)".

authors.name: Tsegaye Gabre-Medhin
authors.bio: Bewketu Seyoum is an Ethiopian writer, poet, essayist, and entertainer. Honored as Ethiopian Best Novelist of the Year in 2008 and Best Young Author in 2009, he is widely regarded as one of the leading poets of his generation. He has published six books of stories, poetry novels, and collections of essays in English and his native language Amarhic. He is also well-known for his comedic writing and performing his short sketches.

authors.name: Yismaeke Werku
authors.bio: Yismaeke Worku is a young Ethiopian author who gained popularity among his readers with his book Dertogada. In addition, Dertogada is the first book in the history of Ethiopian book sales, having been published 10 times in just one year and selling more than 200,000 (two hundred thousand) copies in its first edition. Yismaeke has published fifteen books so far.

authors.name: Baalu Girma
authors.bio: Bealu Girma was an Ethiopian journalist and author known for his criticism of prominent members of the Derg in his book Oromay ("The End"). He was a notable journalist during the time of Emperor Haile Selassie I and served in various media positions in the country. Girma also wrote notable works, including Beyond the Horizon, The Bell of Conscience, The Call of the Red Star, and Haddis.

authors.name: Alemayehu Gelagay
authors.bio: Writer and critic Alemayehu Gelagay was born in 1960 in Arat Kilo. He attended Menelik II Primary and Secondary Schools. He graduated from the Addis Ababa School of Journalism. Alemayehu Gelagay is known for his articles and critiques in various newspapers.

authors.name: Endalegeta Kebede
authors.bio: "Author, poet, game philosopher, journalist, theater expert, all of them come before his name. More than being a captive of "Birhanu Zereihun", a nationalist of "Baalu",  a witness to the value of "Sebhat", I like the name "Endalegeta Kebede"." -Melaku Daniel

authors.name: Tesfaye Gebreab
authors.bio: Tesfaye, who is widely known for his literary works, was born and raised on August 22, 1960, to Eritrean parents in the town of Bishoftu, about 40 kilometers east of Addis Ababa. Tesfaye had a great love and passion for literature, and says that he had close relationships with many famous Ethiopian writers since his youth.

authors.name: Alex Abraham
authors.bio: Alex Abraham is an anonymous contemporary Ethiopian writer and poet known for emotionally rich and culturally grounded storytelling.

authors.name: Bewketu Seyoum
authors.bio: Bewketu Seyoum is an Ethiopian writer and poet from Debre Markos of Ethiopia. He studied psychology at Addis Ababa University and published his first collection of poems, Nwari Alba Gojowoch (Unmanned Houses) in 2000, a year after graduating. Since then, he has published two further poetry collections and two novels, and has narrated short stories on CD. In 2008, he received the best young writer award of Ethiopia from the President. Some of his poetry has appeared in Modern Poetry in Translation, The Big Green Issue (2008), and Callaloo (2011).

authors.name: Azerg
authors.bio: Azerg is an anonymous Ethiopian writer known for his serialized Amharic fiction, most notably the Eyariko series. Writing under a pseudonym, he represents a new generation of digital-era storytellers whose work circulates primarily through online platforms rather than traditional publishing. His identity remains unknown, adding to the intrigue surrounding his writing, which has gained popularity among young Ethiopian readers for its episodic style and relatable themes.

authors.name: Henok Haile
authors.bio: Henok is a journalist in profession and also a preacher & deacon in Ethiopian orthodox church. A graduate from AAU school of journalism. He has published 14 books, two of which are translations from English, while the rest are original works.

authors.name: Zenebe Wela
authors.bio: Zenebe Wela is a prominent Ethiopian author, writer, and media personality known for his contribution to Amharic literature and for encouraging a reading culture. He has authored several books, including "Lijenet" (Childhood), which focuses on childhood memories and experiences.

authors.name: Daniel Kibret
authors.bio: An Ethiopian politician and deacon of the Ethiopian Orthodox Tewahedo Church. Being one of founders of Mahibere Kidusan (a spiritual association in the EOTC), his sermons, in depth writings and powerful oratorical speeches both within and outside the church has attracted millions of Ethiopians domestically and around the world. Having writing 35 books in his over 30 years service, Deacon Daniel Kibret was given the honorary title "Muhaze Tibebat" which is an ancient Ethiopic Ge'ez.

authors.name: Kebede Michael
authors.bio: He was an Ethiopian-born author of fiction and non-fiction. He is considered one of the most accomplished and versatile intellectuals in modern Ethiopia - a poet, playwright, essayist, translator, historian, novelist, philosopher, journalist, and civil servant.

authors.name: Sebhat Gebre-Egziabher
authors.bio: An Ethiopian writer from Tigray Region. He is famous for pioneering the naturalist writing style in Amharic. His writing style was not constrained by the traditional Ethiopian writing style nor orthodox syntax. Despite using simple words and seemingly light prose, Sebhat's concepts are highly sophisticated and philosophical.
